title: The Editor, the Scene Tree, and Nodes as Building Blocks
html:
Welcome to Godot 4

Godot is a free, open-source engine that ships everything you need to build 2D and full 3D games in one ~100MB download. Over this track you will build real games in every major genre — Pong, Breakout, Asteroids, a side-scroller, a top-down Zelda-like, a 3D first-person shooter, a 3D racer, an RPG, and more. Before any of that, you must be fluent in the three ideas that drive everything in the engine: the editor, the scene tree, and nodes.

The editor layout

Open Godot and create a new project. You land in the main editor window. Four regions matter most:

  • Viewport (center) — where you see and arrange your game. Toggle 2D, 3D, and Script with the buttons at the top center.
  • Scene dock (top-left) — the tree of nodes that make up the currently open scene.
  • FileSystem dock (bottom-left) — every file in your project folder (res:// is the project root).
  • Inspector (right) — every editable property of the selected node.

Nodes: the atoms of Godot

A node is a single object that does one job. Sprite2D draws a texture. AudioStreamPlayer plays a sound. Camera3D decides what the player sees. CharacterBody2D moves with collision. There are hundreds, and the engine is essentially a giant family tree of node classes. The base of that family is the class literally named Node; almost everything inherits from it.

You build games by composing nodes: combine simple nodes under a parent and the combination behaves like one richer object. A player might be a CharacterBody2D (movement) with a Sprite2D child (looks), a CollisionShape2D child (hitbox), and an AudioStreamPlayer2D child (footsteps).

The scene tree

When you arrange nodes into a parent-child hierarchy and save it, you have a scene (a .tscn file). A scene always has exactly one root node. Child nodes inherit the position, visibility, and pausing of their parent — move the parent, the children move with it.

At runtime, the engine maintains one live SceneTree: the currently active scenes assembled into a single tree with a special invisible root (the Window/viewport). Understanding this tree is the key to navigating Godot: parents drive children, and you find other nodes by their path within the tree.

Scenes are reusable.
Any scene can be instanced inside another scene, the same way a node is added. A bullet scene, a coin scene, an enemy scene — you build once and stamp out copies. We lean on this constantly starting in lesson 5.

Building your first scene

In the Scene dock, click Other Node, search Node2D, and add it as the root. Rename it Main. With Main selected, add a child Sprite2D. In the Inspector, drag the Godot icon (res://icon.svg) from FileSystem into the Sprite2D's Texture slot. You now see the logo in the viewport. Save with Ctrl+S as main.tscn, then press Play (F5) and set it as the main scene.

The node lifecycle

Every node receives callbacks from the engine at predictable moments. These are virtual methods you override in a script:

CallbackWhen it runs
_enter_tree()The node is added to the tree (before children).
_ready()The node and all its children are in the tree and initialized. Set things up here.
_process(delta)Every rendered frame. delta = seconds since last frame.
_physics_process(delta)Every physics tick (fixed 60/s by default). Use for movement/physics.
_exit_tree()The node leaves the tree.

Attach a script to your Main node (right-click → Attach Script) and try them:

main.gd
# extends tells Godot which node class this script customizes extends Node2D # _ready runs once, after everything is in the tree func _ready() -> void: print("Main is ready! My name is ", name) print("I have ", get_child_count(), " children") # _process runs every frame; delta keeps motion frame-rate independent func _process(delta: float) -> void: # spin the first child (the Sprite2D) 90 degrees per second $Sprite2D.rotation += deg_to_rad(90) * delta

The $Sprite2D syntax is a node path — shorthand for get_node("Sprite2D"), fetching a child by name. Press Play: the logo spins. You just used the lifecycle, the tree, and a node reference together.

Why delta matters.
Without * delta, a fast machine would spin faster than a slow one. Multiplying by delta expresses motion as "per second," so it looks identical everywhere. Memorize this — every game in this track depends on it.
🎮 Build This: Core / Setup — Spinning Logo Scene

This STARTS our journey (a setup exercise, not yet a genre game). You will reuse this exact scene-building flow for every project from here on.

  1. Create a new project and a scene with a Node2D root named Main.
  2. Add a Sprite2D child and assign res://icon.svg as its texture.
  3. Center the sprite in the viewport and save as main.tscn; set it as the main scene.
  4. Attach main.gd using the code above so the sprite spins.
  5. Add a second Sprite2D as a child of the first sprite; offset it and watch it orbit — proving children inherit the parent's transform.
  6. Press F5 to run, then experiment: change deg_to_rad(90) and observe the speed difference.

title: GDScript In Depth — OOP, Custom Classes, Static Typing, and Resources
html:
GDScript: the language of Godot

GDScript is a Python-flavored language built specifically for the engine. It is indentation-based, garbage-collected, and tightly integrated with nodes. This lesson makes you fluent so that every game afterward is about design, not syntax.

Variables and static typing

You can write untyped GDScript, but you should not. Adding type hints gives you editor autocomplete, compile-time error checks, and faster execution. Use := to infer a type from the right-hand side, or : Type to declare explicitly.

types.gd
extends Node var health: int = 100 # explicit type var speed := 250.0 # inferred as float var player_name: String = "Ada" var alive: bool = true var position := Vector2(0, 0) # built-in math types const MAX_LIVES: int = 3 # constant, cannot change # typed array: only floats allowed inside var scores: Array[int] = [10, 20, 30]

Functions

Functions declare typed parameters and a return type after ->. Use void when nothing is returned.

funcs.gd
func take_damage(amount: int) -> void: health -= amount if health <= 0: die() # default parameter value func heal(amount: int = 10) -> int: health = min(health + amount, 100) return health func die() -> void: print(player_name, " has fallen")

Control flow

Standard if/elif/else, for, while, and the powerful match (like switch):

flow.gd
for i in range(3): # 0, 1, 2 print(i) for s in scores: # iterate a collection print(s) match health: 100: print("full") 0: print("dead") _: print("wounded") # _ is the default case

Custom classes with class_name

Every script is already a class. Add class_name at the top and the class becomes a global type usable anywhere — it even appears in the "Create Node" dialog. extends sets the parent class you inherit from.

entity.gd
# a reusable base class for anything with health class_name Entity extends Node2D var max_health: int = 100 var health: int = 100 func take_damage(amount: int) -> void: health = max(health - amount, 0) if health == 0: _on_death() # subclasses override this func _on_death() -> void: queue_free() # remove this node from the tree safely

Inheritance and override

A new class can extends Entity and add or replace behavior. Use super() to call the parent's version.

enemy.gd
class_name Enemy extends Entity var score_value: int = 50 func _on_death() -> void: print("Enemy died, award ", score_value) super() # still run Entity._on_death() to queue_free

Resources: data as objects

A Resource is a serializable data container. Unlike a node, it is not part of the scene tree — it is pure data you can save to disk (.tres), share between scenes, and edit in the Inspector. Resources are how professionals keep data clean and reusable: weapon stats, enemy definitions, dialogue lines, level configs. The @export annotation exposes a property in the Inspector.

weapon_data.gd
class_name WeaponData extends Resource @export var weapon_name: String = "Pistol" @export var damage: int = 10 @export var fire_rate: float = 0.5 # seconds between shots @export var ammo: int = 12

Now any node can hold a WeaponData slot. Right-click in FileSystem → New Resource → WeaponData to create a pistol.tres asset and tune it in the Inspector — no code changes needed.

gun.gd
extends Node2D @export var weapon: WeaponData # drag pistol.tres here in Inspector func _ready() -> void: if weapon: print("Equipped ", weapon.weapon_name, " doing ", weapon.damage)
Nodes vs Resources.
Nodes do things in the scene tree (behavior). Resources hold things (data) and live independent of the tree. When you find yourself copy-pasting numbers between enemies, that data belongs in a Resource.
🎮 Build This: Core / Language — Typed Entity Library

This STARTS your reusable code foundation (a language exercise, not a genre game). The Entity class and Resource pattern return in nearly every later game.

  1. Create entity.gd with class_name Entity and a take_damage method as above.
  2. Create enemy.gd that extends Entity, overrides _on_death, and calls super().
  3. Create a WeaponData Resource class, then make a pistol.tres and a rifle.tres asset with different stats.
  4. Attach gun.gd to a node, export a WeaponData slot, and assign each asset to confirm the printout changes — proving data-driven design works.
  5. Add a typed Array[WeaponData] on the gun and loop over it in _ready.

title: Pong — Scripts, _process, and Input
html:
Your first finished game

Time to ship something. Pong is the perfect first game: two paddles, one ball, two scores. Building it cements scripts, the frame loop, input handling, and basic collision response — the bones of every game.

The Input Map

Hard-coding keys is fragile. Godot's Input Map lets you define named actions and bind keys to them. Go to Project → Project Settings → Input Map and add four actions: p1_up, p1_down, p2_up, p2_down. Bind W/S to player 1 and the Up/Down arrows to player 2. In code you then ask Input.is_action_pressed("p1_up") — and could later rebind keys without touching scripts.

Scene setup

Create main.tscn with a Node2D root named Main. Add three children, each a Sprite2D with a simple colored texture (use the icon, or set a ColorRect-style texture): PaddleLeft, PaddleRight, and Ball. Position the paddles near the left and right edges, the ball in the center. For crisp arcade collisions we will treat each as a simple rectangle and do the math ourselves.

The paddle script

Each paddle reads its two actions and moves vertically, clamped to the screen. We use @export so the same script serves both paddles with different action names.

paddle.gd
extends Sprite2D @export var up_action: String = "p1_up" @export var down_action: String = "p1_down" @export var speed: float = 400.0 func _process(delta: float) -> void: var dir := 0.0 if Input.is_action_pressed(up_action): dir -= 1.0 if Input.is_action_pressed(down_action): dir += 1.0 position.y += dir * speed * delta # keep paddle inside the 648px-tall screen (with margins) position.y = clampf(position.y, 40.0, 608.0)

Attach this to PaddleLeft with the default actions. For PaddleRight, attach it too and change the exports in the Inspector to p2_up / p2_down. One script, two configurations — your first taste of reuse.

The ball script

The ball stores a velocity vector and moves every frame. When it crosses the top or bottom, it bounces by flipping velocity.y. When it overlaps a paddle, it flips velocity.x. When it leaves the left or right edge, someone scores and the ball resets. We grab paddle references in _ready and use simple rectangle overlap tests.

ball.gd
extends Sprite2D @export var speed: float = 350.0 var velocity := Vector2.ZERO var half := Vector2(16, 16) # half the ball size var paddle_half := Vector2(12, 48) @onready var left: Sprite2D = $"../PaddleLeft" @onready var right: Sprite2D = $"../PaddleRight" func _ready() -> void: reset_ball(1) func reset_ball(direction: int) -> void: position = Vector2(576, 324) # screen center var angle := randf_range(-0.5, 0.5) velocity = Vector2(direction, sin(angle)).normalized() * speed func _process(delta: float) -> void: position += velocity * delta # bounce off top and bottom walls if position.y < half.y or position.y > 648 - half.y: velocity.y = -velocity.y # paddle collisions if _overlaps(left) and velocity.x < 0: velocity.x = -velocity.x if _overlaps(right) and velocity.x > 0: velocity.x = -velocity.x # scoring if position.x < 0: get_parent().add_score(2) reset_ball(1) elif position.x > 1152: get_parent().add_score(1) reset_ball(-1) func _overlaps(paddle: Sprite2D) -> bool: return abs(position.x - paddle.position.x) < half.x + paddle_half.x \ and abs(position.y - paddle.position.y) < half.y + paddle_half.y
@onready explained.
@onready delays a variable's assignment until _ready() runs, so the node path is valid (the tree is built). Without it, $"../PaddleLeft" would run too early and crash.

Scorekeeping in Main

The Main script owns the two scores and gets called by the ball. Add a Label child named ScoreLabel to display them.

main.gd
extends Node2D var score1: int = 0 var score2: int = 0 @onready var label: Label = $ScoreLabel func add_score(player: int) -> void: if player == 1: score1 += 1 else: score2 += 1 label.text = "%d : %d" % [score1, score2]
Why _process and not _physics_process?
Pong's collision is hand-rolled math, not Godot physics bodies, so the per-frame _process loop is fine. Once we use real physics bodies (lesson 6), movement belongs in _physics_process for a stable fixed timestep.
🎮 Build This: Classic Arcade — Pong

This STARTS a new genre: classic arcade. It is your first complete, playable game and your first finished project of the track.

  1. Add the four input actions in Project Settings → Input Map.
  2. Build main.tscn with two paddles, a ball, and a ScoreLabel.
  3. Attach paddle.gd to both paddles; set player 2's exports to p2_up/p2_down.
  4. Attach ball.gd and main.gd; wire the score label.
  5. Play it two-player. Then tune: increase ball speed slightly each paddle hit for rising tension.
  6. Stretch goal: make the bounce angle depend on where the ball hits the paddle (top vs bottom) for skillful aiming.

title: Signals, Decoupled Architecture, and Breakout
html:
Events, not polling

In Pong the ball reached into Main with get_parent().add_score(). That works, but it tightly couples the ball to its parent — move the ball into a different scene and it breaks. Professional Godot uses signals: a node announces "something happened" and any interested node listens. The announcer doesn't know or care who listens. This is event-driven, decoupled design, and it scales to huge games.

What a signal is

A signal is a named event a node can emit. Other nodes connect a function to it. When emitted, every connected function runs. Built-in nodes already have signals (a Button emits pressed); you can also declare your own.

signal_basics.gd
extends Node # declare a custom signal that carries a value signal brick_destroyed(points: int) func smash() -> void: # announce it; we don't know who is listening brick_destroyed.emit(100)

Connecting signals

You can connect in the editor (Node dock → Signals tab) or in code. In code, connect to a Callable — a reference to a method:

connecting.gd
func _ready() -> void: # when the brick emits, call our _on_brick_destroyed $Brick.brick_destroyed.connect(_on_brick_destroyed) func _on_brick_destroyed(points: int) -> void: add_score(points)

Real collision with Area2D

Breakout needs real collision detection, so we graduate from hand-math to physics areas. An Area2D is a node that detects overlaps without simulating physics. Give it a CollisionShape2D child (a RectangleShape2D) and it emits area_entered / body_entered signals when something overlaps. We will make the ball a CharacterBody2D for clean wall bouncing and the bricks Area2Ds for detection.

The brick

Each brick is an Area2D with a CollisionShape2D and a Sprite2D/ColorRect. It declares a signal and frees itself when the ball touches it.

brick.gd
extends Area2D signal destroyed(points: int) @export var points: int = 100 func _ready() -> void: body_entered.connect(_on_body_entered) func _on_body_entered(body: Node2D) -> void: if body.is_in_group("ball"): destroyed.emit(points) queue_free()

Put the ball in a group named ball (Node dock → Groups tab, or add_to_group("ball") in its _ready). Groups let many nodes share a tag without referencing each other.

The ball with physics movement

A CharacterBody2D uses move_and_collide, which returns collision info we use to bounce off walls and the paddle. We bounce by reflecting the velocity across the collision normal.

ball.gd
extends CharacterBody2D @export var speed: float = 400.0 func _ready() -> void: add_to_group("ball") velocity = Vector2(randf_range(-0.5, 0.5), 1).normalized() * speed func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: var collision := move_and_collide(velocity * delta) if collision: # reflect velocity across the surface normal -> a clean bounce velocity = velocity.bounce(collision.get_normal())
Groups vs direct references.
is_in_group("ball") lets the brick react to any ball without holding a hard reference. Combined with signals, your objects stop knowing about each other's internals — that is decoupling.

Main wires it together

Main spawns/holds the bricks and connects each one's destroyed signal to a scorer. Because bricks announce via signal, Main never has to poll them — it just listens.

main.gd
extends Node2D var score: int = 0 var bricks_left: int = 0 @onready var label: Label = $ScoreLabel func _ready() -> void: for brick in get_tree().get_nodes_in_group("bricks"): brick.destroyed.connect(_on_brick_destroyed) bricks_left += 1 func _on_brick_destroyed(points: int) -> void: score += points bricks_left -= 1 label.text = "Score: %d" % score if bricks_left == 0: label.text = "You win! Score: %d" % score

Add every brick to a bricks group so Main can find them all at once. Notice Main and Brick never call each other's methods directly — the signal is the only link. Swap, duplicate, or rearrange bricks freely; nothing else changes.

The decoupling rule.
Prefer signals up (child tells parent something happened) and direct calls down (parent commands child). When a child reaches up with get_parent(), ask whether a signal would be cleaner. It usually is.
🎮 Build This: Classic Arcade — Breakout

This STARTS a new game in the classic arcade genre. It reuses the paddle idea from Pong but replaces hand-math with real Area2D/CharacterBody2D collision and signal-driven scoring.

  1. Build a paddle (reuse Pong's paddle.gd, horizontal this time) controlled by left/right input.
  2. Make a brick.tscn (Area2D + CollisionShape2D + ColorRect) with brick.gd; add it to the bricks group.
  3. Lay out a grid of brick instances across the top of the screen.
  4. Build the CharacterBody2D ball that bounces with move_and_collide and joins the ball group.
  5. In main.gd, connect every brick's destroyed signal and tally the score.
  6. Add walls (StaticBody2D) on three sides; lose a life when the ball falls past the paddle.

title: Scene Instancing, Autoloads, and Snake
html:
Stamping out copies at runtime

So far you placed nodes by hand in the editor. Real games create objects while running — bullets, enemies, coins, and, in Snake, a body segment for every apple eaten. The mechanism is scene instancing: save a scene as a reusable template (a PackedScene), then call instantiate() to make live copies. You will use this in every remaining game.

PackedScene and instantiate

Build a small scene (say segment.tscn) and save it. To spawn one in code, preload the scene file and instantiate it, then add the instance to the tree:

spawning.gd
extends Node2D # preload bakes the scene into the script at load time const SEGMENT := preload("res://segment.tscn") func spawn_at(grid_pos: Vector2) -> Node2D: var seg := SEGMENT.instantiate() # a fresh live copy add_child(seg) # now it exists in the tree seg.position = grid_pos return seg
preload vs load.
preload(path) resolves at compile time (fast, path must be a constant). load(path) resolves at runtime (use when the path is dynamic). For a known asset, prefer preload.

Autoloads: global singletons

Some data must outlive any single scene — the score, the high score, settings, the current level. An Autoload (singleton) is a scene or script the engine loads once at startup and keeps alive for the whole game, accessible by a global name everywhere. Set it in Project → Project Settings → Globals → Autoload.

game_manager.gd
# register this as autoload named "Game" extends Node signal score_changed(new_score: int) signal game_over var score: int = 0 var high_score: int = 0 func add_score(amount: int) -> void: score += amount high_score = max(high_score, score) score_changed.emit(score) func reset() -> void: score = 0 score_changed.emit(score)

After registering it as Game, any script anywhere can call Game.add_score(10) or connect to Game.score_changed. Combining autoloads with signals gives you a clean global event bus — no fragile node paths across scenes.

Snake on a grid

Snake lives on a discrete grid and steps on a timer, not every frame. We track the head, a direction, and an ordered list of body segments. Each step: compute the new head cell, move forward, and either grow (ate food) or drop the tail.

snake.gd
extends Node2D const SEGMENT := preload("res://segment.tscn") const CELL := 32 # pixels per grid cell const STEP := 0.15 # seconds between moves var dir := Vector2.RIGHT var next_dir := Vector2.RIGHT var body: Array[Node2D] = [] # [0] is the head var timer := 0.0 var grow := false func _ready() -> void: _add_segment(Vector2(5, 5)) _add_segment(Vector2(4, 5)) _add_segment(Vector2(3, 5)) func _process(delta: float) -> void: _read_input() timer += delta if timer >= STEP: timer = 0.0 _step() func _read_input() -> void: # prevent reversing directly into yourself if Input.is_action_pressed("ui_up") and dir != Vector2.DOWN: next_dir = Vector2.UP elif Input.is_action_pressed("ui_down") and dir != Vector2.UP: next_dir = Vector2.DOWN elif Input.is_action_pressed("ui_left") and dir != Vector2.RIGHT: next_dir = Vector2.LEFT elif Input.is_action_pressed("ui_right") and dir != Vector2.LEFT: next_dir = Vector2.RIGHT func _step() -> void: dir = next_dir var head_cell := body[0].position / CELL var new_cell := head_cell + dir # self-collision = game over for seg in body: if seg.position == new_cell * CELL: Game.game_over.emit() return var new_head := _make_segment(new_cell) body.insert(0, new_head) if grow: grow = false else: var tail: Node2D = body.pop_back() tail.queue_free() func _add_segment(cell: Vector2) -> void: body.append(_make_segment(cell)) func _make_segment(cell: Vector2) -> Node2D: var seg := SEGMENT.instantiate() add_child(seg) seg.position = cell * CELL return seg func eat() -> void: grow = true Game.add_score(10)

The food is its own small scene with an Area2D; when the head overlaps it, call snake.eat() and reposition the food to a random free cell. Every new segment is an instance of segment.tscn — instancing is the engine of growth.

Timer-based stepping.
Snake feels right because it moves in discrete cells at a fixed cadence, not smoothly each frame. Accumulating delta into a timer and acting when it crosses STEP is a pattern you will reuse for cooldowns, spawns, and turn timers everywhere.
🎮 Build This: Classic Arcade — Snake

This STARTS a new game in the classic arcade genre, and introduces two tools used in every later genre: runtime instancing and an autoload game manager.

  1. Register game_manager.gd as the Game autoload singleton.
  2. Create segment.tscn (a 32x32 ColorRect) and a food.tscn (Area2D + shape).
  3. Add snake.gd; grow the body by instancing segment.tscn on each apple.
  4. Detect self-collision and emit Game.game_over; score via Game.add_score.
  5. Build a HUD Label that connects to Game.score_changed and updates live.
  6. On game over, show the high score (stored in the autoload, so it survives a restart).

title: 2D Physics Bodies, Areas, Layers & Asteroids
html:
Letting the engine simulate physics

Until now you moved objects by editing position directly. Asteroids is the moment to hand momentum and collisions to Godot's physics engine. You will learn the three physics body types, collision layers and masks, and screen-wrapping — the full toolkit for 2D physics games.

The three 2D bodies

BodyMoves howUse for
StaticBody2DNever (immovable)Walls, floors, fixed obstacles
RigidBody2DFull physics simulation (forces, mass, gravity)Asteroids, debris, anything tumbling
CharacterBody2DYou move it via move_and_slidePlayer-controlled characters

Asteroids float with momentum and bounce off each other — perfect for RigidBody2D. The ship is best as a RigidBody2D too (it has drift/inertia), or a CharacterBody2D if you prefer tight control. We will use a RigidBody2D ship for authentic Newtonian feel.

Collision layers and masks

Every physics body sits on one or more layers and scans one or more masks. A body on layer X is detected only by bodies whose mask includes X. This is how you say "bullets hit asteroids but not the ship." Name your layers in Project Settings → Layer Names → 2D Physics, e.g. 1 = Ship, 2 = Asteroid, 3 = Bullet.

Layer vs Mask rule.
Layer = "what I am." Mask = "what I look for." Body A detects body B only if A's mask overlaps B's layer. Set a bullet's layer to Bullet and its mask to Asteroid, and it will collide with asteroids while ignoring the ship entirely.

The ship: thrust and rotation

A RigidBody2D is moved by applying forces, not by setting position. We rotate with torque-free direct rotation for crisp control, and thrust forward by applying a force along the ship's facing. Set the ship's Gravity Scale to 0 in the Inspector (no gravity in space).

ship.gd
extends RigidBody2D @export var thrust: float = 600.0 @export var turn_speed: float = 4.0 const BULLET := preload("res://bullet.tscn") func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: # rotate the body directly if Input.is_action_pressed("ui_left"): rotation -= turn_speed * delta if Input.is_action_pressed("ui_right"): rotation += turn_speed * delta # apply thrust along facing direction if Input.is_action_pressed("ui_up"): apply_central_force(Vector2.RIGHT.rotated(rotation) * thrust) if Input.is_action_just_pressed("ui_accept"): _shoot() _wrap_screen() func _shoot() -> void: var b := BULLET.instantiate() get_parent().add_child(b) b.global_position = global_position b.launch(Vector2.RIGHT.rotated(rotation))

Screen wrapping

Asteroids' signature is toroidal space: leave the right edge, reappear on the left. We wrap any body by modulo-ing its position against the viewport size.

ship.gd (continued)
func _wrap_screen() -> void: var size := get_viewport_rect().size if global_position.x < 0: global_position.x += size.x elif global_position.x > size.x: global_position.x -= size.x if global_position.y < 0: global_position.y += size.y elif global_position.y > size.y: global_position.y -= size.y

The bullet with Area2D

Bullets are cheap and many, so a lightweight Area2D is ideal — it detects overlaps without full physics. Set its mask to the Asteroid layer.

bullet.gd
extends Area2D @export var speed: float = 700.0 var velocity := Vector2.ZERO func launch(direction: Vector2) -> void: velocity = direction.normalized() * speed get_tree().create_timer(2.0).timeout.connect(queue_free) func _ready() -> void: area_entered.connect(_on_hit) func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: position += velocity * delta func _on_hit(area: Area2D) -> void: if area.is_in_group("asteroids"): area.call("split") queue_free()

Splitting asteroids

A big asteroid hit spawns two smaller ones, the heart of Asteroids' escalation. The asteroid is a RigidBody2D with random initial linear_velocity; split() instances two children of the next size down, then frees itself.

asteroid.gd
extends RigidBody2D const ASTEROID := preload("res://asteroid.tscn") @export var size_level: int = 2 # 2 big, 1 medium, 0 small func _ready() -> void: add_to_group("asteroids") linear_velocity = Vector2.RIGHT.rotated(randf() * TAU) * randf_range(40, 120) scale = Vector2.ONE * (0.4 + size_level * 0.3) func split() -> void: if size_level > 0: for i in 2: var a := ASTEROID.instantiate() a.size_level = size_level - 1 get_parent().add_child(a) a.global_position = global_position Game.add_score(100) queue_free()
RigidBody and your own movement.
Never set a RigidBody's position directly every frame to drive it — that fights the simulation. Use forces/impulses, or switch to CharacterBody2D. Screen-wrapping is an exception: it is a one-off teleport, acceptable as long as it is rare.
🎮 Build This: Classic Arcade — Asteroids

This STARTS a new game in the classic arcade genre and is your deep dive into Godot's 2D physics: RigidBody2D, Area2D, and collision layers/masks.

  1. Name 2D physics layers: Ship, Asteroid, Bullet. Configure each scene's layer and mask accordingly.
  2. Build the RigidBody2D ship with thrust, rotation, and screen wrapping (gravity scale 0).
  3. Make bullet.tscn (Area2D) that frees itself after 2 seconds and splits asteroids on hit.
  4. Make asteroid.tscn (RigidBody2D) with three size levels that split into two smaller ones.
  5. Spawn a wave of large asteroids at start; clear the wave to trigger the next.
  6. Reuse the Game autoload from Snake for scoring; end the game if an asteroid hits the ship.

title: Control Nodes, Audio Buses, and Space Invaders
html:
Polish: UI and sound

A game needs a face and a voice. Space Invaders is the perfect capstone for the arcade era: it needs a proper HUD (lives, score, wave), formation enemy movement, and satisfying audio. You will learn Control nodes (Godot's UI system) and audio buses (a mixing console for sound), then tie them into a real game.

Control nodes and anchors

UI in Godot uses Control nodes, separate from the Node2D world. They live under a CanvasLayer so they stay fixed on screen regardless of camera movement. Key ones: Label (text), TextureRect (images), Button, and containers like HBoxContainer/VBoxContainer that auto-arrange children. The magic of Controls is anchors: a Control anchors to edges/corners of its parent, so the UI adapts to any window size. Set anchors via the toolbar's anchor presets (e.g., "Top Wide" for a header bar).

hud.gd
extends CanvasLayer @onready var score_label: Label = $Margin/HBox/ScoreLabel @onready var lives_label: Label = $Margin/HBox/LivesLabel func _ready() -> void: Game.score_changed.connect(_on_score_changed) _on_score_changed(Game.score) func _on_score_changed(value: int) -> void: score_label.text = "SCORE %06d" % value func set_lives(n: int) -> void: lives_label.text = "LIVES %d" % n
CanvasLayer keeps UI fixed.
Put your HUD under a CanvasLayer node. It draws on top of the game world and ignores the Camera2D, so the score never scrolls away. The world (player, enemies) stays under your normal Node2D tree.

Audio buses

Open the Audio panel at the bottom of the editor. Every sound routes through the Master bus by default. Add buses — say SFX and Music — and route each to Master. Now you can set per-bus volume, mute, solo, and add effects (reverb, compressor) to a whole category at once. An AudioStreamPlayer picks its bus via its Bus property.

audio.gd
extends Node @onready var shoot_sfx: AudioStreamPlayer = $ShootSFX # Bus = SFX func play_shoot() -> void: shoot_sfx.play() # adjust an entire bus at runtime (e.g., from a settings slider) func set_sfx_volume(linear: float) -> void: var bus := AudioServer.get_bus_index("SFX") AudioServer.set_bus_volume_db(bus, linear_to_db(linear))

The enemy formation

Invaders move as one block: the whole grid shifts sideways, and when any edge enemy hits the wall, the entire formation drops down and reverses. The cleanest design is a formation manager that owns all enemies and moves them together on a step timer (reusing the timer pattern from Snake).

formation.gd
extends Node2D const INVADER := preload("res://invader.tscn") @export var cols: int = 11 @export var rows: int = 5 @export var spacing := Vector2(64, 48) var direction: float = 1.0 # 1 right, -1 left var step_time: float = 0.5 var timer: float = 0.0 func _ready() -> void: for r in rows: for c in cols: var inv := INVADER.instantiate() add_child(inv) inv.position = Vector2(c, r) * spacing inv.tree_exited.connect(_on_invader_freed) func _process(delta: float) -> void: timer += delta if timer >= step_time: timer = 0.0 _step() func _step() -> void: var hit_edge := false var margin := 40.0 var width := get_viewport_rect().size.x for inv in get_children(): var x := inv.global_position.x if (x > width - margin and direction > 0) or (x < margin and direction < 0): hit_edge = true if hit_edge: direction *= -1.0 position.y += spacing.y # drop the whole block down else: position.x += spacing.x * 0.25 * direction func _on_invader_freed() -> void: # speed up as the swarm thins (classic difficulty curve) step_time = max(0.1, step_time - 0.01)

Each invader is an Area2D that emits when a player bullet hits it (same pattern as Breakout's bricks). The formation node moving its own position drags all child invaders along, because children inherit the parent transform — the scene tree doing the heavy lifting for free.

The player and shooting

The player ship moves left/right and fires bullets upward; invaders periodically drop bombs downward. Lives decrement on a hit, updating the HUD via the autoload signal. When get_child_count() on the formation hits zero, the wave is cleared — spawn the next, faster wave.

One pattern, many games.
Notice how much carries over: the timer-step from Snake, the Area2D + signal collision from Breakout, instancing from lesson 5, and the Game autoload for score. Space Invaders is mostly assembly of tools you already own — that is the payoff of learning fundamentals first.
🎮 Build This: Classic Arcade — Space Invaders

This STARTS a new game and CLOSES the classic arcade foundation. It combines instancing, signals, timers, the score autoload, plus the new UI and audio systems.

  1. Add audio buses SFX and Music; route invader-shoot, explosion, and music to them.
  2. Build a CanvasLayer HUD with anchored Labels for score, lives, and wave.
  3. Create invader.tscn (Area2D) and a formation.gd manager that marches and drops the swarm.
  4. Build a player ship that fires bullets upward; connect invader hits to scoring and audio.
  5. Speed the formation up as invaders die; advance to a new wave when the formation empties.
  6. Lose a life when an invader bomb hits the player; game over at zero lives via the HUD and Game.game_over.

title: CharacterBody2D, Tilemaps & a Side-Scrolling Platformer
html:
Entering the platformer genre

You have mastered arcade games where the whole screen fits in view. Platformers introduce three new pillars: CharacterBody2D for responsive gravity-and-jump movement, TileMapLayer for painting big levels efficiently, and a Camera2D that follows the hero through a scrolling world. Get these right and you can build Mario, Celeste, or Hollow Knight.

CharacterBody2D and move_and_slide

CharacterBody2D is the body type for player-controlled characters. You set its velocity, call move_and_slide(), and Godot handles collision response — sliding along walls and floors instead of sticking or tunneling. It also tracks whether you are on the ground via is_on_floor().

Gravity, run, and jump

The core platformer loop: apply gravity every physics tick, read horizontal input for running, and add an upward velocity burst on jump (only when grounded). Read the project's default gravity so it matches engine settings.

player.gd
extends CharacterBody2D @export var speed: float = 250.0 @export var jump_velocity: float = -480.0 # negative = up # pull the project's gravity setting (pixels/sec^2) var gravity: float = ProjectSettings.get_setting("physics/2d/default_gravity") func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: # 1. gravity is always pulling unless grounded if not is_on_floor(): velocity.y += gravity * delta # 2. jump only when standing on something if Input.is_action_just_pressed("jump") and is_on_floor(): velocity.y = jump_velocity # 3. horizontal run from -1..1 var dir := Input.get_axis("move_left", "move_right") if dir != 0.0: velocity.x = dir * speed else: # decelerate smoothly when no key is held velocity.x = move_toward(velocity.x, 0.0, speed) # 4. let Godot move us and resolve collisions move_and_slide()
Input.get_axis is gold.
get_axis("move_left", "move_right") returns -1, 0, or 1 in one call, and works seamlessly with analog gamepad sticks for free. Define move_left, move_right, and jump in the Input Map (bind WASD/arrows and Space).

TileMapLayer: painting levels

Hand-placing hundreds of platform sprites is unthinkable. A TileMapLayer lets you paint a grid of tiles from a TileSet — and bake collision into the tiles so the whole level is one collidable object. (In Godot 4.3+, TileMapLayer replaces the older single TileMap node; you stack multiple layers for background/foreground/collision.)

  1. Add a TileMapLayer node to your scene.
  2. In the Inspector, create a new TileSet and drag in a tileset image (the atlas).
  3. In the bottom TileSet panel, add a Physics Layer, then paint collision polygons onto your solid tiles (the editor auto-fits a square with one click).
  4. Switch to the TileMap panel and paint your level directly in the viewport.

Because the collision is part of the tile data, your CharacterBody2D collides with the painted ground automatically — no per-tile nodes, no extra scripting. One TileMapLayer can hold an entire world.

TileMap layers, plural.
Use separate TileMapLayer nodes for distinct jobs: one for decorative background (no collision), one for solid terrain (with a physics layer), one for hazards. Layering keeps editing clean and lets you control draw order and collision independently.

A follow camera

Attach a Camera2D as a child of the player and it tracks the player automatically. Enable Position Smoothing in the Inspector for a smooth lag, and set Limits (left/right/top/bottom) so the camera never scrolls past the level edges — a hallmark of polished platformers. You can also use a separate camera with manual follow code:

follow_camera.gd
extends Camera2D @export var target_path: NodePath @export var smooth: float = 5.0 @onready var target: Node2D = get_node(target_path) func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: if target: # lerp toward the target for buttery follow global_position = global_position.lerp(target.global_position, smooth * delta)

Collectibles and goal

Coins are Area2Ds in a group that emit when the player's body enters them — the exact Breakout/Invaders pattern, now in a scrolling world. The level-end flag is another Area2D that loads the next scene with get_tree().change_scene_to_file("res://level2.tscn").

coin.gd
extends Area2D func _ready() -> void: body_entered.connect(_on_body_entered) func _on_body_entered(body: Node2D) -> void: if body.is_in_group("player"): Game.add_score(10) queue_free()
Why _physics_process for movement.
All character motion and camera-follow live in _physics_process, the fixed-timestep loop. This keeps physics deterministic and prevents jitter where rendering and collision disagree. Keep gameplay physics here; keep purely visual effects in _process.
🎮 Build This: Platformer — Side-Scroller

This STARTS a new genre: the platformer. It is the foundation you will EXTEND in the next lessons (animation, then a full metroidvania), so build it cleanly.

  1. Add Input Map actions: move_left, move_right, jump (keyboard + gamepad).
  2. Build a CharacterBody2D player with the gravity/run/jump script; add it to a player group.
  3. Create a TileMapLayer with a TileSet, add a physics layer, and paint a level with gaps and platforms.
  4. Add a smoothed Camera2D with limits set to the level bounds.
  5. Scatter coin.tscn Area2D collectibles that score via the Game autoload.
  6. Place a goal flag Area2D that calls change_scene_to_file to load the next level.
